q: What are the three categories of data structure — structured, semi-structured, and unstructured — and how do they differ in storage and querying?
a: Structured data has a rigid, predefined schema with rows and columns — every record conforms to the same field layout. It is stored in relational tables and queried with SQL. Classic examples are customer records, order lines, and financial transactions. Semi-structured data has flexible or self-describing structure where schema varies per record; it uses tags, keys, or markers to organize data without a fixed table. JSON, XML, CSV with inconsistent columns, and Avro fall here — they can be stored in document stores or data lakes and queried with tools like Azure Synapse serverless pools. Unstructured data has no inherent schema at all: images, video, audio, PDF documents, and raw log text. It lives in blob/object stores (Azure Blob Storage) and requires AI/ML pipelines to extract meaning. The distinction matters for choosing the right Azure service: Azure SQL Database for structured, Azure Cosmos DB for semi-structured, and Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 for unstructured at scale. Roughly 80% of the world's data is unstructured, which is why lake-first architectures have grown dominant.
fj: CSV files are structured data because they have consistent column headers.
fs: CSVs are semi-structured at best. There is no enforced schema — columns can be missing, data types are not declared, and different rows can carry different fields. A relational table with enforced types and constraints is structured; a CSV file is a loosely formatted text file that must be parsed and validated before it can be treated as structured.

q: A junior analyst asks why Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 stores both IoT sensor readings (JSON) and raw camera images together. How do you explain structured vs unstructured storage strategy?
a: A data lake is designed to hold all three data categories in their native format so that transformation can happen downstream on demand. The IoT JSON readings are semi-structured: each message has named fields (device_id, timestamp, temperature) but the set of optional fields can vary by sensor model. The camera images are unstructured: there is no schema — they are binary blobs of pixel data. Storing both in ADLS Gen2 preserves raw fidelity, avoids up-front schema commitment, and lets different consumers (a SQL analyst using serverless Synapse for the JSON, an ML team using PyTorch for the images) each access and transform what they need independently. If you only loaded the JSON into a relational database and discarded the images, you would permanently lose unstructured signal that future computer-vision models might need. The ETL philosophy has shifted from 'transform before loading' to 'land everything, transform on read'.
fj: Only structured data should be stored in a data lake; unstructured files belong in a separate file server.
fs: This reverses the purpose of a data lake. Data lakes are specifically designed to hold raw data of all three types at low cost. Forcing a structure-only policy defeats the lake's value proposition and often destroys data that becomes valuable later (e.g., unstructured call-centre audio fed into speech analytics a year after collection).

q: Compare CSV, JSON, Parquet, Avro, and ORC as data file formats. When would you choose each in an Azure data pipeline?
a: CSV is a plain-text row format with comma-separated values. It is human-readable and universally supported but has no schema, no compression, and is slow for analytical queries — best for small interchange files or source system exports. JSON is a self-describing semi-structured row format ideal for APIs and document data. It handles nested objects and arrays natively but is verbose and lacks columnar compression. Parquet is a columnar binary format with built-in compression (Snappy, GZIP) and column pruning. It is the default format for Azure Synapse Analytics and Azure Databricks analytics jobs because queries that touch only a few columns skip entire column groups, dramatically reducing I/O. Avro is a row-based binary format that bundles schema inside the file header. It excels at write-heavy streaming workloads (Azure Event Hubs Capture uses Avro) and schema evolution because the schema travels with the data. ORC (Optimized Row Columnar) is a columnar format analogous to Parquet, most common in Hadoop/Hive ecosystems; you may encounter it in migrations from on-premises Hadoop to Azure. Decision rule: real-time event streaming → Avro; analytical querying at scale → Parquet; simple data exchange → CSV/JSON.
fj: Parquet is best for streaming pipelines because it is compressed and fast.
fs: Parquet is optimised for read-heavy analytical queries, not streaming writes. Its columnar layout requires buffering an entire row group before writing, which adds latency incompatible with streaming. Avro is the streaming-optimised format — its row-based layout allows record-by-record serialisation, and Azure Event Hubs Capture uses Avro by default for exactly this reason.

code.label: Read Parquet with column pruning (Azure Databricks)
lines: # Only reads 'device_id' and 'temperature' columns from disk
# OtherColumnGroups are skipped entirely — columnar advantage

q: What is the difference between relational and non-relational (NoSQL) databases, and how do Azure services map to each?
a: A relational database organises data into tables with rows and columns. Relationships between tables are enforced through primary and foreign keys, and the schema is defined up front. Relational databases guarantee ACID transactions (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) and use SQL as the query language. They excel at structured business data where referential integrity and complex joins matter — Azure SQL Database, Azure Database for PostgreSQL, and Azure Database for MySQL are Azure's relational offerings. Non-relational (NoSQL) databases relax the fixed schema requirement to handle diverse, high-velocity, or massive-scale data. They trade some ACID guarantees for horizontal scalability and schema flexibility. NoSQL databases come in four major models: document (Azure Cosmos DB Core/SQL API stores JSON documents), key-value (Azure Table Storage maps a key to an arbitrary value bag), wide-column (Cosmos DB Cassandra API), and graph (Cosmos DB Gremlin API for nodes and edges). The right choice depends on data shape: structured, related entities → relational; high-throughput flexible documents → Cosmos DB; simple fast lookups → Azure Table Storage.
fj: Non-relational databases cannot support any form of query language, only key lookups.
fs: This is a common misconception. Azure Cosmos DB's Core (SQL) API supports a SQL-like syntax for querying JSON documents. MongoDB API supports MQL. Even Azure Table Storage supports OData filter queries. 'Non-relational' means 'not organised as joined tables' — it does not mean 'query-free'.

q: Describe the distinct responsibilities of a database administrator, a data engineer, and a data analyst in a modern Azure data team.
a: A database administrator (DBA) owns the health, security, and availability of database systems. Their day-to-day covers provisioning and patching Azure SQL Database instances, managing logins and role-based access, monitoring query performance and tuning indexes, setting up geo-redundancy and automated backups, and responding to on-call incidents. They ensure the database is running and trustworthy — not what the data means. A data engineer designs and builds the data pipelines and infrastructure that move and transform data at scale. In Azure they wire together Azure Data Factory pipelines, configure Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 zones, build Synapse Analytics workspaces, and author Spark jobs or dbt models. They are responsible for data reliability, schema design, and making clean data available to consumers. A data analyst uses the data prepared by engineers to answer business questions. They write SQL queries, build Power BI dashboards, and produce reports that guide decisions. They focus on insight extraction — trend analysis, KPI tracking, and storytelling with data — not on pipeline plumbing. In small teams the roles overlap, but conceptually: DBA = keep systems running, data engineer = move and transform data, data analyst = interpret and communicate findings.
fj: A data engineer and a data analyst are interchangeable roles — both write SQL and work with databases.
fs: While both roles use SQL, the scope differs fundamentally. A data engineer builds the pipelines, schemas, and infrastructure that deliver data — they are concerned with reliability, latency, throughput, and data quality at the platform level. A data analyst consumes that prepared data to answer specific business questions and build dashboards. Conflating them leads to analysts being pulled into plumbing work or engineers being judged on business insight they were not hired to produce.

q: A team merges the DBA and data engineer roles to save headcount. What risks arise and which Azure features can mitigate them?
a: When one person owns both database operations and pipeline development, two risk categories emerge. First, context-switching degradation: DBA work is reactive and interruption-driven (on-call incidents, performance firefighting), while data engineering requires sustained focus for building complex pipelines. Merging them means neither receives adequate attention. Second, single-point-of-failure: if this person leaves or is on holiday, both production database health and pipeline delivery stop. Azure mitigations: Azure SQL Database's built-in automated backups, active geo-replication, and threat detection reduce the manual DBA burden significantly — the platform handles patching, HA, and backup retention by default. Azure Monitor and Intelligent Insights alert on performance regressions without someone watching graphs continuously. For pipelines, Azure Data Factory's monitoring and alerting, along with Git-backed source control, reduce the bus-factor risk. In practice, managed PaaS databases (Azure SQL Database over SQL Server on VM) are the standard recommendation for small teams precisely because they offload DBA undifferentiated heavy lifting to Microsoft.
fj: Using managed PaaS databases eliminates the need for any DBA or operations knowledge.
fs: PaaS offloads infrastructure maintenance (patching, hardware, OS), but design decisions — index strategy, query plan analysis, RBAC, connection pool sizing, disaster recovery testing, and schema evolution — still require database expertise. 'Managed' means the platform runs the engine; it does not mean the platform designs your schema or responds to a runaway query in production.

q: What are the key structural features of relational data — tables, primary keys, foreign keys, and indexes — and why does each matter?
a: A table is a two-dimensional structure of rows and columns where each column has a defined data type and each row represents one entity instance. Tables are the atomic unit of a relational schema. A primary key (PK) is a column or combination of columns whose value uniquely identifies each row in a table; it cannot be NULL and must be unique. The PK is the canonical way to retrieve a specific record. A foreign key (FK) is a column in one table that references the PK of another table, enforcing referential integrity — the database rejects an INSERT or UPDATE that would create a dangling reference. This is what makes data 'relational': the Orders table has a customer_id FK pointing to the Customers PK, so every order is guaranteed to link to a valid customer. An index is a separate data structure (typically a B-tree) that the database engine maintains alongside the table to speed up lookups. A primary key automatically creates a clustered index in most engines. Additional non-clustered indexes on frequently filtered columns (e.g., order_date) prevent full table scans. The trade-off: indexes speed reads but slow writes because the index must be maintained on every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE.
fj: A foreign key automatically creates an index on the referencing column in Azure SQL Database.
fs: Foreign key constraints enforce referential integrity but do not automatically create an index on the referencing column in SQL Server or Azure SQL Database. Best practice is to manually add an index on FK columns because FK lookups (e.g., finding all orders for a given customer_id) are very common, and without an index they cause full table scans. Forgetting this is a frequent performance bug.

code.label: Create table with PK, FK, and index (Azure SQL)
lines: customer_id   INT           NOT NULL IDENTITY(1,1) PRIMARY KEY,
order_id     INT  NOT NULL IDENTITY(1,1) PRIMARY KEY,
-- Best practice: index the FK column to avoid full scans

q: Explain the difference between DDL and DML SQL statements, and give the core examples of each that appear on DP-900.
a: DDL (Data Definition Language) statements define or modify the structure of database objects. They operate on the schema, not on the data inside tables. The four core DDL verbs are CREATE (creates a new table, index, view, or database), ALTER (modifies an existing object, e.g., adds a column), DROP (permanently removes an object), and TRUNCATE (removes all rows from a table by deallocating data pages — faster than DELETE, no row-by-row logging, and can be rolled back inside an explicit transaction in SQL Server and Azure SQL Database, unlike in MySQL where TRUNCATE issues an implicit commit and cannot be rolled back). DML (Data Manipulation Language) statements operate on the data stored inside tables. The four core DML verbs are SELECT (retrieves rows — the most common operation), INSERT (adds new rows), UPDATE (modifies existing rows), and DELETE (removes rows one-by-one with full logging and can be rolled back in a transaction). For DP-900 the key distinction is: DDL changes what the database looks like (structure), DML changes what the database contains (data). A DBA typically controls DDL permissions tightly; analysts are usually granted only SELECT; data engineers need INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE for pipeline operations.
fj: TRUNCATE is a DML statement because it removes data from a table.
fs: TRUNCATE is DDL in most SQL engines (including SQL Server / Azure SQL Database) because it operates at the allocation level, dropping and re-creating data pages rather than logging individual row deletions. This means it cannot be filtered by a WHERE clause, is transactional in SQL Server and Azure SQL Database — it can be rolled back inside an explicit transaction, and bypasses triggers. DELETE is the DML equivalent that removes rows one at a time with full logging and supports WHERE filtering.

code.label: DDL vs DML examples (Azure SQL)
lines: CREATE TABLE Products (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name NVARCHAR(100));
ALTER TABLE Products ADD price DECIMAL(10,2);
INSERT INTO Products (id, name, price) VALUES (1, 'Widget', 9.99);
SELECT id, name FROM Products WHERE price < 20;
UPDATE Products SET price = 12.99 WHERE id = 1;

q: Describe the three Azure managed relational database services for open-source engines — Azure Database for PostgreSQL, Azure Database for MySQL, and Azure Database for MariaDB — and when you would choose each.
a: Azure Database for PostgreSQL is a fully managed PaaS service for the PostgreSQL engine. It comes in two deployment modes: Flexible Server (current recommended mode — single-region or zone-redundant HA, read replicas, point-in-time restore) and the legacy Single Server (being retired). PostgreSQL is preferred for complex analytical queries, JSON document support via jsonb, geospatial queries via PostGIS, and workloads migrating from on-premises Postgres. Azure Database for MySQL is a fully managed PaaS service for the MySQL engine, also available as Flexible Server. It is the go-to for LAMP-stack applications, WordPress, and Drupal migrations to Azure. MySQL has a simpler feature set than PostgreSQL and is widely used in web applications. Azure Database for MariaDB is a managed service for the MariaDB fork of MySQL. It is targeted at organisations that standardised on MariaDB before Microsoft acquired it or that need MariaDB-specific features like the Aria storage engine. Microsoft has announced that Azure Database for MariaDB will be retired in September 2025, encouraging migration to Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server. All three offload patching, backups, HA, and hardware management to Microsoft, following the managed PaaS model.
fj: Azure Database for PostgreSQL and Azure Database for MySQL are identical services that differ only in name.
fs: They run fundamentally different database engines with different SQL dialects, feature sets, extension ecosystems, and performance characteristics. PostgreSQL supports advanced features like partial indexes, table inheritance, and the jsonb type for document queries. MySQL has broader web-application adoption and different storage engine options. Migrating an application from one to the other requires schema and query changes — they are not drop-in replacements.

q: You are migrating a WordPress site from on-premises MySQL 8.0 to Azure. Which Azure relational service do you choose, and what connection-string change is required?
a: The correct choice is Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server because WordPress requires MySQL and the Flexible Server deployment mode is the current recommended option, offering zone-redundant HA, up to 99.99% SLA, read replicas, and a maintenance window you can schedule. The migration path is: export the on-premises database with mysqldump, import into an Azure MySQL Flexible Server instance, and then update the WordPress wp-config.php file with the new host, user, password, and SSL configuration. Azure Database for MySQL enforces SSL/TLS connections by default; the connection string must include ssl-mode=REQUIRED and point to the DigiCert root CA bundle downloaded from Microsoft. The host changes from your on-premises IP to the Azure FQDN of the form your-server-name.mysql.database.azure.com. No changes to the WordPress application code are needed if the MySQL version is compatible. Azure Database for MariaDB was also a valid historical choice but is being retired in September 2025, so it should not be selected for new deployments.
fj: You should use Azure SQL Database because it is the primary Azure relational service and supports all SQL workloads.
fs: Azure SQL Database runs the Microsoft SQL Server engine, not MySQL. WordPress uses MySQL-specific syntax and the PDO_MYSQL or mysqli PHP drivers. Pointing it at Azure SQL Database would cause driver incompatibility errors immediately. The correct mapping is: MySQL workloads → Azure Database for MySQL; PostgreSQL workloads → Azure Database for PostgreSQL; SQL Server workloads → Azure SQL Database.

code.label: wp-config.php snippet for Azure MySQL Flexible Server
lines: // Azure MySQL requires SSL — add to MYSQL_CLIENT_FLAGS

q: What is Azure File Storage, what protocols does it support, and what are its primary use cases versus Azure Blob Storage?
a: Azure Files is a managed file share service that presents cloud storage as a network file system accessible via standard protocols. It supports two protocols: SMB (Server Message Block) — versions 2.1, 3.0, and 3.1.1 — which is the Windows file-sharing standard, mountable from Windows, Linux, and macOS clients; and NFS (Network File System) — version 4.1 — which is the UNIX/Linux file-sharing standard for Linux-only workloads. Azure Files is designed for lift-and-shift scenarios where applications expect a file system rather than an object API — legacy apps that call fopen() or reference UNC paths (\\server\share\file.txt) cannot use Blob Storage but work immediately when Azure Files is mounted as a drive. Common use cases: shared configuration files accessible by multiple VMs, on-premises file server replacement, home directories for Azure Virtual Desktop users, and SMB shares for SQL Server database files. The contrast with Azure Blob Storage: Blob is an object store accessed via REST API or SDK, optimised for storing massive unstructured data (images, video, backups) at the lowest cost per GB; it does not mount as a drive. Azure Files costs more per GB than Blob but provides the OS-level file system semantics that some applications require.
fj: Azure Blob Storage can be mounted as a network drive using SMB, making Azure Files unnecessary.
fs: Azure Blob Storage does not natively support SMB mounting. Azure Files is the service specifically designed for SMB and NFS file share semantics. There is a separate feature called BlobFuse (a FUSE driver for Linux) that exposes Blob as a file system, but it is not SMB, has performance trade-offs, and is not the same as Azure Files. For applications requiring true SMB/NFS share semantics, Azure Files is the correct choice.

q: Describe Azure Table Storage — its data model, access pattern, and when it is appropriate versus Azure Cosmos DB Table API.
a: Azure Table Storage is a key-value NoSQL store that holds schema-less rows of data. Each row is identified by a composite key of PartitionKey + RowKey; the PartitionKey groups rows that are stored and queried together, and the RowKey is a unique identifier within that partition. Beyond those two keys, each row can have any number of arbitrary properties — there is no enforced schema, so row A in the same table can have a 'colour' property while row B does not. This schema-less flexibility makes it ideal for heterogeneous entity data (e.g., storing different event types with different attributes in one table). Azure Table Storage is cheap (it uses the same Azure Storage account as Blob and File) and scales to petabytes, making it suitable for simple high-volume lookups: IoT device metadata, user session data, audit logs, or game leaderboards. The limitation is that it only supports queries on PartitionKey and RowKey efficiently; filtering on arbitrary properties causes full partition or table scans. Azure Cosmos DB Table API is the premium upgrade path: it offers the same key-value API with automatic global distribution, single-digit millisecond latency SLAs, and secondary indexing on all properties, at higher cost. DP-900 rule of thumb: Table Storage for cost-sensitive simple key lookups, Cosmos DB Table API when you need global scale, low-latency SLAs, or richer querying.
fj: Azure Table Storage supports complex multi-column joins like a relational database because it uses a 'table' structure.
fs: Despite the word 'table', Azure Table Storage is a key-value NoSQL store with no join capability. The word refers to the logical container for rows, not to a relational table. There are no foreign keys, no referential integrity, and no JOIN syntax — a query returns rows matching PartitionKey/RowKey filters only. Joins must be handled in application code by making multiple requests.

code.label: Insert and query Azure Table Storage (azure-data-tables SDK)
lines: # Query by PK only — efficient partition scan

q: Azure Cosmos DB supports five APIs. Name each, its underlying data model, and the migration scenario it targets.
a: Azure Cosmos DB is a globally distributed, multi-model database that exposes the same underlying infrastructure through five wire-protocol-compatible APIs. Core (SQL) API is the native API: stores JSON documents, queried with a SQL-like syntax, and is the default choice for new Cosmos DB projects. It has no migration legacy — use it for greenfield document workloads. MongoDB API supports the MongoDB wire protocol so existing MongoDB drivers (PyMongo, Mongoose) connect without code changes; targets teams migrating from MongoDB Atlas or self-hosted MongoDB who want Azure-managed infrastructure. Cassandra API implements the Cassandra Query Language (CQL) and the CQL binary protocol; targets wide-column workloads migrating from Apache Cassandra or DataStax on-premises, preserving existing drivers and CQL queries. Gremlin API implements the Apache TinkerPop Gremlin graph traversal language; targets graph workloads — social networks, fraud detection, recommendation engines — where entities (vertices) and relationships (edges) are first-class objects. Table API provides a premium, globally distributed upgrade path for applications already using Azure Table Storage, preserving the same SDK but adding SLA-backed single-digit millisecond latency and automatic secondary indexes. A sixth API, PostgreSQL API (powered by Citus), was added later for distributed Postgres but is less prominent on DP-900.
fj: The Cosmos DB MongoDB API stores data in BSON format, identical to a real MongoDB server.
fs: Cosmos DB's MongoDB API stores data in its own internal format and translates MongoDB wire protocol at the service layer — it is not actually running MongoDB. The data is stored in Cosmos DB's proprietary format under the hood, not BSON on disk. This means some advanced MongoDB features (transactions across collections, full aggregation pipeline compatibility, certain index types) may have limitations or behave differently from native MongoDB. Microsoft publishes a compatibility matrix for each MongoDB API version supported.

q: You have an existing fraud-detection application that queries relationships between accounts, devices, and transactions using Apache Gremlin. How does migrating to Azure Cosmos DB Gremlin API preserve your code?
a: Azure Cosmos DB Gremlin API implements the Apache TinkerPop 3.x Gremlin traversal language and accepts connections from any TinkerPop-compliant Gremlin driver — Python's gremlinpython, Java's gremlin-driver, or .NET's Gremlin.NET. Because the wire protocol and query language are preserved, the traversal queries your fraud application already uses (e.g., g.V().has('account','id','ACC-001').out('SENT_TO').in('USED_DEVICE').values('ip')) continue to work after pointing the connection string at Cosmos DB. The migration benefit is operational: you swap self-managed TinkerPop/JanusGraph infrastructure for a fully managed, globally replicated, SLA-backed PaaS service. Cosmos DB handles partitioning of the graph using a partition key you specify, and it auto-scales throughput via Request Units (RU/s). The architectural consideration is choosing the right partition key — in a fraud graph, partitioning by account region or time bucket avoids hot partitions. Cross-partition graph traversals still work but consume more RUs, so query patterns should inform partitioning strategy.
fj: You must rewrite all Gremlin queries as SQL queries when migrating to Cosmos DB because Cosmos DB only supports SQL.
fs: Cosmos DB is a multi-model database with multiple API endpoints. The Gremlin API endpoint speaks native TinkerPop Gremlin — no SQL conversion is needed. The Core (SQL) API is just one of five APIs. You connect to the Gremlin endpoint specifically, and your existing Gremlin drivers and traversal queries continue to work. Rewriting to SQL would be necessary only if switching from the Gremlin API to the Core (SQL) API, which would also change the data model from graph to document.

code.label: Connect to Cosmos DB Gremlin API (gremlinpython)
lines: # Same Gremlin traversal — no rewrite needed

q: What is the difference between ETL and ELT, and how does Azure Data Factory support both patterns?
a: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) transforms data before it reaches the destination. Data is extracted from sources, cleaned and reshaped in a transformation engine (historically a dedicated ETL server), and only then loaded into the target database or warehouse. ETL was dominant when storage was expensive and target systems were underpowered — you only loaded clean, correct data. ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) loads raw data into the destination first and then transforms it in place using the destination's own compute power. This pattern has become standard with cloud data warehouses (Azure Synapse Analytics, Snowflake) that have massive parallel processing (MPP) engines capable of transforming terabytes quickly. Loading raw data first also preserves full history and allows multiple downstream transformations from the same raw source. Azure Data Factory supports both patterns: its data flows and mapping transformations run on Spark clusters inside ADF, enabling ETL without loading raw data. Alternatively, ADF can use the Copy Activity to land data in ADLS Gen2 or Synapse (load first), then a subsequent Stored Procedure Activity or Synapse Notebook activity performs transformation in Synapse — this is ELT. The choice depends on whether the target system or ADF's Spark engine is more cost-efficient for the transformation workload.
fj: Azure Data Factory only supports ETL; for ELT you need a separate tool like dbt.
fs: Azure Data Factory explicitly supports both patterns in a single service. The Copy Activity + downstream Synapse/Databricks activity enables ELT by loading first and transforming in the destination engine. ADF's Mapping Data Flows run Spark-backed transformations inside ADF, enabling ETL. dbt is a popular transformation layer that complements ADF (running SQL-based transformations in Synapse/Databricks) but ADF itself is not restricted to ETL.

q: You are designing a pipeline that loads raw JSON events from an IoT device fleet into Azure Synapse Analytics for daily reporting. Walk through an ELT architecture using Azure Data Factory.
a: Step 1 — Extract: Configure an ADF Linked Service pointing to the IoT device data source (e.g., Azure Event Hubs with Capture enabled, writing Avro files to ADLS Gen2). Step 2 — Load (raw zone): Use an ADF Copy Activity to move the raw Avro files to the ADLS Gen2 raw zone. This step does no transformation — the goal is to land everything. Step 3 — Load (curated zone): Schedule a second ADF pipeline that runs daily. It triggers an Azure Synapse Notebook (or a Synapse Spark job) that reads the raw Avro, parses the JSON payload, flattens nested fields, casts data types, filters malformed rows, and writes the result as Parquet to the curated zone of ADLS Gen2. Step 4 — Load to warehouse: A final ADF Copy Activity or Synapse COPY INTO statement loads the curated Parquet into a Synapse dedicated SQL pool fact table. Power BI then connects to Synapse for reporting. The architecture is ELT because raw data lands in the lake before any transformation occurs, and the transformation runs inside Synapse's Spark engine — not in ADF's own compute. Monitoring: ADF Pipeline runs dashboard + Azure Monitor alerts on failures.
fj: You should use an ADF Mapping Data Flow for all transformations so the raw data never needs to touch ADLS Gen2.
fs: Transforming entirely in ADF Mapping Data Flows (ETL pattern) loses the raw-zone preservation benefit. If the transformation logic has a bug, the raw data is gone. The ELT pattern deliberately keeps the raw layer immutable so you can re-run transformations from scratch at any time without re-extracting from the source. For IoT pipelines where source re-extraction is impractical or impossible, preserving the raw zone is an operational necessity.

q: Compare data lakes, data warehouses, and lakehouses as analytical data stores. What Azure service represents each pattern?
a: A data lake stores raw data in its native format (structured, semi-structured, and unstructured) in cheap object storage with no enforced schema. It is optimised for large-scale ingestion and flexibility — you land everything and decide how to use it later. On Azure, Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS Gen2) is the storage layer, often paired with Azure Databricks or Synapse Spark for processing. Weakness: no query performance guarantees, no ACID transactions on files, data quality is the user's responsibility. A data warehouse stores cleaned, structured, schema-on-write data optimised for analytical SQL queries. It uses columnar storage and MPP (massively parallel processing) for fast aggregate queries. On Azure, Azure Synapse Analytics dedicated SQL pools and Azure SQL Data Warehouse are the data warehouse offerings. Weakness: expensive to store raw data; schema must be designed up front; loading unstructured data is impractical. A lakehouse is a modern hybrid that adds data warehouse features (ACID transactions, schema enforcement, indexing, query optimization) directly on top of a data lake storage layer. It uses open table formats — Delta Lake (the Azure Databricks default), Apache Iceberg, or Apache Hudi — to bring warehouse reliability to lake economics. On Azure, Azure Databricks with Delta Lake and Azure Synapse Analytics' Lake Database feature implement the lakehouse pattern. The lakehouse is the emerging standard for new architectures.
fj: A data warehouse and a lakehouse are the same thing — both use SQL and store structured data.
fs: A traditional data warehouse enforces schema on write (data must be structured before loading) and stores data in proprietary formats on managed compute+storage that you cannot access independently. A lakehouse stores data in open formats (Parquet + Delta Lake transaction log) on open object storage (ADLS Gen2) that can be queried by multiple engines. The lakehouse also handles semi-structured and evolving schemas with schema evolution support, which a classic data warehouse cannot do without expensive ALTER TABLE operations.

q: What is the difference between batch and streaming data processing, and which Azure services support each?
a: Batch processing collects data over a period of time — minutes, hours, or days — and processes it as a single job at a scheduled interval. The entire dataset (or a large partition of it) is available when processing begins. Classic examples: nightly ETL jobs that load the previous day's transactions into a data warehouse, monthly billing runs, weekly model retraining. Azure services: Azure Data Factory pipelines, Azure Synapse Analytics Spark pools, Azure Databricks jobs, and Azure HDInsight (Hadoop-based) all support batch processing. Latency is measured in minutes to hours. Streaming (real-time) processing ingests and processes data continuously, event by event or in micro-batches of seconds, as it arrives. The output is updated in near-real-time. Examples: fraud detection acting on each credit card transaction as it occurs, dashboard metrics updating every 10 seconds, IoT anomaly alerts triggered within 1 second. Azure services: Azure Stream Analytics (SQL-based, fully managed), Azure Event Hubs (ingestion), Azure Databricks Structured Streaming, and Azure Synapse Analytics real-time analytics support streaming. Latency is measured in milliseconds to seconds. The choice is driven by acceptable latency and cost: streaming requires always-on compute; batch is cheaper but stale. Many architectures use both — a Lambda or Kappa architecture serves historical (batch) and live (streaming) views simultaneously.
fj: Azure Stream Analytics is used for batch processing large historical datasets because it is a managed analytics service.
fs: Azure Stream Analytics is specifically a streaming analytics service designed for continuous, real-time event processing using a SQL-like query language over live data streams from Event Hubs or IoT Hub. It is not designed for batch historical processing of static datasets. For batch analytics over stored data, use Azure Synapse Analytics, Azure Databricks, or Azure Data Factory. Stream Analytics processes data in motion; Synapse processes data at rest.

code.label: Azure Stream Analytics — tumbling window over Event Hub stream
lines: -- Stream Analytics query: count transactions per merchant
-- every 60 seconds using a Tumbling Window

q: An e-commerce company needs to detect fraudulent orders within 2 seconds of purchase. Should they use batch or streaming processing, and which Azure service best fits?
a: This requires streaming processing because the acceptable latency is 2 seconds — batch jobs run on schedules (minutes to hours) and cannot meet sub-second to low-second detection requirements. The correct Azure service is Azure Stream Analytics combined with Azure Event Hubs as the ingestion layer. The architecture: the e-commerce platform publishes each purchase event to an Event Hub topic the moment it is placed. Stream Analytics applies a real-time SQL query — joining the event stream against a reference data set of known fraudulent patterns, flagging orders where transaction velocity exceeds a threshold (e.g., 3+ orders from the same device in 60 seconds using a Sliding Window), or where billing and shipping countries differ. Flagged events are routed to an Azure Service Bus queue that triggers a downstream fraud-review microservice, which can hold the order before fulfilment. The key Stream Analytics features used: reference data joins (for static blacklist lookups), windowed aggregations (Tumbling or Sliding windows for velocity checks), and multiple outputs (one to Power BI for a live dashboard, one to Service Bus for the action queue). Azure Databricks Structured Streaming is a valid alternative for teams that need Python/ML-based detection logic beyond what SQL windows can express.
fj: Run a batch job every 5 minutes to scan new orders — 5-minute latency is acceptable for fraud detection.
fs: Five-minute latency means a fraudulent order can be fulfilled and shipped before detection fires. For fraud that involves digital goods (software keys, gift cards) or same-day delivery, 5 minutes is far too slow. Real fraud detection systems require sub-second to low-second response — precisely the use case for streaming. The 5-minute batch approach was the pre-streaming era compromise; it is not acceptable architecture for modern fraud pipelines.

q: Match these five visualization types — bar chart, line chart, map, scatter plot, and KPI card — to their best analytical use case in a Power BI report.
a: A bar chart (or column chart) compares discrete categories at a point in time. Use it to compare sales revenue across five product lines, or headcount across departments — the axis encodes category, the bar length encodes value. A line chart shows change over continuous time. Use it to track daily active users over a quarter, or monitor CPU utilisation over hours — the x-axis is time, the y-axis is the metric, and trend and seasonality are immediately visible. A map visualization plots data geographically. Use it to show sales density by region, customer location clusters, or logistics route coverage — spatial patterns invisible in a table become obvious on a map. Power BI supports filled maps (choropleth), bubble maps, and ArcGIS maps. A scatter plot (scatter chart) shows the correlation between two continuous numeric variables. Use it to investigate whether ad spend correlates with revenue, or whether order size correlates with delivery delay — each dot is an entity, x and y are the two variables, and clustering or outliers reveal relationships. A KPI card (Card visual or KPI visual) displays a single key metric, often compared to a target. Use it for a live header showing 'Revenue: $2.4M vs $2.1M target (+14%)'. It answers 'are we on track?' in a single glance. DP-900 tip: the exam often tests whether you can identify which visual answers a specific business question — match the shape of the question (comparison/trend/geography/correlation/status) to the visual type.
fj: A scatter plot is the best way to show sales performance over time because it can plot many data points.
fs: A scatter plot encodes the relationship between two numeric variables — it is designed for correlation analysis, not time-series trends. Using it for time-series would plot time on one axis and the metric on the other, which technically works but is inferior to a line chart because the line chart's connected data points explicitly encode temporal order and continuity. For time-based trend analysis, a line chart is always the correct choice.
